title: How Financial Trauma Affects Your Ability to Charge What You're Worth
body:
Financial Trauma and Your Worth

You're probably undercharging. You're probably afraid to ask for what you deserve, to name your price, to stand firm when someone tries to haggle you down. And it's not because you lack business savvy or negotiation skills. It's because your past money experiences, the ones you've shoved deep down, are actively sabotaging your present and future earning potential.

Honestly, you think it's about the market, about competition, about what people will "pay." But it's not. It's about what you believe you're worth. And that belief, my friend, is often a direct product of financial trauma.

What is Financial Trauma, Really?

Here's the thing, when I say "financial trauma," I'm not just talking about losing everything in a stock market crash or going through bankruptcy. While those are certainly traumatic, financial trauma is far more insidious, far more common, and far more subtle than you might imagine.

It's the constant stress of growing up poor. It's watching your parents fight about money, the hushed tones, the slammed doors. It's being told "we can't afford that" so often that it became your internal mantra. It's the shame of needing help, the guilt of wanting more, the fear of never having enough. It's the times you felt powerless, helpless, or deeply ashamed because of money, or the lack thereof.

Maybe you had a parent who gambled away savings. Maybe you were burdened with adult financial responsibilities as a child. Maybe you witnessed a loved one lose their home. These aren't just "bad memories." These are experiences that wired your nervous system to associate money with danger, scarcity, and pain. Your brain, in its infinite wisdom, decided that the best way to protect you from that pain again was to keep you small, keep you playing safe, and definitely keep you from asking for too much. Because asking for too much, having too much, could lead to losing it all. And that's terrifying.

Notice what your body does when you think about your debt. Does your stomach clench? Does your chest tighten? Do you feel a wave of anxiety? That's your nervous system reacting, not to a number, but to the unresolved emotional charge tied to that number. It's the echo of past financial pain.

This isn't some woo-woo concept. This is neuroscience. When you experience trauma, your brain creates pathways that prioritize survival. When those pathways are linked to money, every financial decision becomes a potential threat. You become risk-averse, not because you're cautious, but because you're terrified. You undervalue yourself, not because you don't believe in your skills, but because deep down, you're trying to avoid the perceived danger of having "too much" or the pain of rejection if you ask for what you truly want.

Dave Ramsey, for all his good intentions, often misses this crucial piece. He prescribes a one-size-fits-all, shame-based approach to money management, assuming everyone operates from a logical, rational place. But you don't. Your financial decisions aren't always logical. They're often driven by deeply ingrained emotional responses to past experiences. You feel that?

The Self-Sabotage Cycle: Why You Can't Charge What You're Worth

So, how does this play out when you're trying to set your prices, pitch a client, or ask for a raise?

  • The "Too Much" Syndrome: You've been conditioned to believe that wanting more is greedy, selfish, or impossible. You feel guilty even thinking about a higher number. Your inner critic screams, "Who do you think you are?"
  • The Fear of Rejection: If you ask for a higher price, they might say no. And that "no" doesn't just feel like a business rejection; it feels like a personal rejection, echoing past experiences of not being "enough" or not being valued.
  • The Scarcity Mindset: You see money as a finite resource. If you ask for more, someone else gets less. If you make more, it's just a matter of time before it's taken away. This drives you to accept less than you deserve, just to "get the gig."
  • The Imposter Syndrome: You downplay your skills, your experience, your value. You tell yourself you're not good enough, not experienced enough, not qualified enough to charge premium rates. This is often a protective mechanism, a way to avoid the perceived scrutiny that comes with higher stakes.
  • The Martyr Complex: You believe you have to work harder, longer, for less, to prove your worth. You sacrifice your well-being, your time, your energy, all in the name of "earning it."

Know what I mean? It's a vicious cycle. You undercharge, you feel resentful, you burn out, you doubt your abilities, and you continue to undercharge. You're stuck in a loop, and the key to breaking free isn't a new marketing strategy; it's healing your relationship with money.

Bari Tessler, in her book I Will Teach You to Be Rich (paid link) (though the title is Ramit Sethi's, Tessler's work in "The Art of Money" really dives into the emotional side), and Kate Northrup, in "Money: A Love Story," both emphasize that your financial life is deeply intertwined with your emotional life. You can't separate them. Your feelings about money dictate your actions with money, and those actions dictate your results.

What if your relationship with money is the most honest relationship you have? It shows you exactly where you're holding back, where you're afraid, where you're not trusting yourself.

Healing the Wounds: Practical Steps to Reclaim Your Worth

This isn't a quick fix. It's a journey of self-discovery, compassion, and consistent practice. But you can start today.

1. Acknowledge and Validate Your Past

You can't heal what you don't acknowledge. Take time to reflect on your money story. What were your earliest memories of money? What did your parents say or do about money? What financial struggles or anxieties did you witness or experience? Don't judge these memories; just observe them. Journal about them. Talk about them with a trusted friend or therapist.

Understand that your reactions, your fears, your avoidance - they all make sense given what you've been through. You're not "bad" with money; you're just reacting to old wounds. This isn't about blaming anyone; it's about understanding the roots of your current financial patterns.

2. Befriend Your Nervous System

Your body holds the score. When you think about money, especially challenging money situations, pay attention to the physical sensations. Is it a knot in your stomach? A tightness in your jaw? Shortness of breath? These are signs that your nervous system is in a state of alert.

Sam Harris, through his work on mindfulness and meditation, teaches us the power of observing our internal states without judgment. When you feel that anxiety, instead of pushing it away, try to sit with it. Breathe into it. Remind yourself that you are safe now. This isn't about magically making the feeling disappear; it's about creating space around it, so it doesn't control you. Over time, you'll teach your body that money doesn't always equal danger.

3. Redefine "Worth" Beyond the Dollar Sign

Your worth isn't tied to your income, your assets, or your debt. Your worth is inherent. You are worthy simply because you exist. This isn't just a feel-good platitude; it's a fundamental truth that you need to internalize.

Start practicing affirmations of self-worth that have nothing to do with money. "I am worthy of love." "I am capable." "I deserve to thrive." Say them, write them, feel them. The more you connect to your inherent worth, the less you'll rely on external validation (like a paycheck) to define it.

4. Practice Radical Self-Compassion

You've been hard on yourself, haven't you? For not making more, for past financial mistakes, for feeling stuck. Stop. You wouldn't talk to a child that way. Extend the same kindness to yourself.

Kristin Neff's work on self-compassion is real here. When you catch yourself in a cycle of self-criticism around money, pause. Place a hand on your heart. Acknowledge the pain or fear you're feeling. Remind yourself that suffering is part of the human experience, and you're not alone in these struggles. This practice helps to soothe your nervous system and create a safe internal space for healing.

I highly recommend reading Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. While not explicitly about money, its teachings on embracing all parts of yourself, including your pain and fear, are incredibly applicable to financial healing.

5. Get Real with Your Numbers (Gently)

Avoidance is a common trauma response. You might avoid looking at your bank account, opening bills, or even thinking about your prices. This keeps you stuck in the dark.

Start small. Pick one financial number that brings up anxiety. Maybe it's your credit card balance, maybe it's the income you want to make, maybe it's the price you want to charge. Let's sit with the discomfort of that number for a moment. Just observe. Breathe. You don't have to fix it right now. You just have to look at it.

Then, start researching. What are people in your field actually charging? Not just the average, but the top earners. This isn't about comparison in a negative way; it's about expanding your sense of what's possible. The Psychology of Money by Morgan Housel (The Psychology of Money (paid link)) helps demystify many common financial behaviors, but it's crucial to remember that our personal psychology around money runs deeper than just rational decision-making.

6. Practice Asking for More (Small Steps)

You don't have to jump from charging $50 to $500 overnight. Start with micro-experiments.

  • Next time you're offered a discount, ask for a slightly better one.
  • If someone asks for your price, add 10% to what you were originally thinking. See what happens.
  • Practice saying "no" to things that don't align with your value or worth, even if it's just saying "no" to an extra task at work that's outside your job description.
  • Practice negotiating for something small, like a better rate on your internet bill.

Each small win builds confidence and rewires your brain to associate asking for more with positive outcomes, rather than danger.

Beyond the Transaction: Money as a Spiritual Practice

You're not just trying to make more money. You're trying to heal your relationship with a fundamental force in your life. Money, at its core, is energy. It's a tool, a medium of exchange, a reflection of value. When you approach it from a place of fear and lack, you repel it. When you approach it from a place of worthiness, abundance, and service, you attract it.

This isn't about manifesting riches through positive thinking alone. It's about aligning your inner world with your outer actions. It's about doing the deep, messy, uncomfortable work of facing your past so you can build a future where you're not just surviving, but truly thriving.

Does that land? It's not easy. It requires courage. But the alternative is to stay stuck, to keep undercharging, to keep feeling resentful, and to keep letting old wounds dictate your financial reality. And you deserve more than that. You've earned the right to heal.

FAQ

How long does it take to heal financial trauma?

There's no set timeline for healing financial trauma. It's a deeply personal journey, and it often unfolds in layers. You might experience significant shifts in a few months, but deeper patterns can take years to fully integrate. The key is consistent effort, self-compassion, and patience. Don't expect a quick fix; commit to the process.

Do I need a therapist to heal financial trauma?

While not strictly necessary for everyone, a therapist specializing in trauma or financial therapy can be incredibly helpful. They can provide a safe space to explore your past, identify coping mechanisms, and guide you through healing techniques. If your financial trauma feels overwhelming, or if it's deeply intertwined with other past traumas, professional support is highly recommended. For many, however, self-guided work with resources like books, journaling, and mindfulness can be a powerful starting point.

What if I don't remember specific financial traumas?

You don't need to have explicit memories of traumatic events to be affected by financial trauma. Often, it's the subtle, repeated messages and experiences from childhood that shape our money beliefs. Pay attention to your current feelings and reactions around money. If you feel anxiety, fear, shame, or scarcity, those feelings are clues. Journaling about your earliest money memories, even if they seem mundane, can reveal underlying patterns. Your body often remembers what your conscious mind has forgotten.

Is it selfish to want to charge more?

Absolutely not. This belief is a common manifestation of financial trauma. When you charge what you're truly worth, you're not only valuing your time, skills, and expertise, but you're also able to serve your clients better. When you're well-compensated, you can invest in your business, your education, and your well-being, which ultimately allows you to provide higher quality work. Charging your worth is an act of self-respect and a foundation for sustainable impact.

Your challenge today: Identify one small area where you've been undercharging or undervaluing yourself. Write down the number you *wish* you could charge, even if it feels outrageous. Just write it down. Let it sit there. Don't do anything else with it yet. Just acknowledge that desire.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti

author: Kalesh
